quick_facts.label: Award and structure
quick_facts.value: MBBS is the undergraduate medical qualification; the current NMC curriculum structure is 4½ academic years (54 months) followed by one year of compulsory rotating medical internship (CRMI).

quick_facts.label: Primary entrance route
quick_facts.value: Admission to undergraduate medical seats is through NEET (UG); the NTA’s 2026 bulletin states that admissions to all undergraduate medical/dental seats are made through NEET (UG).

quick_facts.label: School-level preparation
quick_facts.value: The standard qualifying route includes Class 12/equivalent study of Physics, Chemistry, Biology or Biotechnology, and English, including practical work where applicable; the 2026 bulletin also recognizes specified alternative/equivalent qualifying routes.

quick_facts.label: Age and NEET qualification context
quick_facts.value: For NEET (UG) 2026, candidates had to complete 17 years by December 31, 2026, with no upper age limit. For MBBS admission, the published minimum qualifying percentile was 50th for General/General-EWS, 40th for SC/ST/OBC-NCL, and specified PwBD thresholds; admission still depends on rank, counselling, seat availability, and applicable rules.

quick_facts.label: Post-MBBS clinical progression
quick_facts.value: NEET-PG is the single eligibility-cum-ranking examination for MD/MS and PG Diploma admissions. NBEMS states that eligible candidates hold a recognized MBBS degree or provisional pass certificate, appropriate registration, and completed—or will complete—one year of internship by the stated cut-off.

sections.heading: Eligibility: academic preparation and NEET requirements
paragraphs: For the usual Indian-school route, plan for Class 12/equivalent with Physics, Chemistry, Biology or Biotechnology, and English. The NEET (UG) 2026 bulletin permits candidates awaiting their Class 12 result to sit NEET, but requires them to pass the qualifying examination before first-round counselling to be admitted. It also recognizes certain equivalent and alternative routes, so applicants educated through non-standard, foreign, or post-Class-12 subject combinations should check the exact qualifying-examination code and produce required equivalency evidence.

For the 2026 cycle, NEET eligibility required completion of age 17 by December 31, 2026; NTA reported no upper age limit. Clearing the NEET qualifying percentile is necessary but is not a seat guarantee: counselling authorities allot seats using All India Rank, applicable category rules, verified documents, choices, and available seats.
bullets: Typical prerequisite subjects: Physics, Chemistry, Biology/Biotechnology, and English.
Confirm the year-specific NEET bulletin before applying: eligibility provisions, categories, document requirements, dates, and cut-offs can change.
For international-board, OCI, NRI, or foreign-national applicants, eligibility is subject to the applicable government, institutional, and counselling rules in addition to NEET requirements.

sections.heading: Duration and internship
paragraphs: The prescribed MBBS pathway comprises 54 months of academic study followed by a one-year compulsory rotating medical internship (CRMI), commonly described as a 5.5-year course. The internship is an integral training stage rather than an additional taught academic year.

In an April 7, 2026 clarification, NMC stated that charging MBBS tuition for five or 5.5 years is inconsistent with the prescribed academic structure, because the teaching period is 54 months and is followed by CRMI. Students should nevertheless confirm each college’s published fee schedule, hostel charges, bond conditions, stipend arrangements, and any state-specific requirements before accepting a seat.
bullets: Practical training period: 1 year of CRMI.
Allow for the possibility that examination timing, university calendars, or supplementary examinations can affect an individual student’s completion timeline.

sections.heading: What students study
paragraphs: NMC’s Competency Based Medical Education (CBME) Curriculum 2024 organizes training across professional phases with early clinical exposure, clinical postings, family-adoption/community activities, and the Attitude, Ethics and Communication (AETCOM) module. The programme begins with a foundation course and progressively moves from biomedical sciences to clinical disciplines and patient care.

The early phase includes anatomy, physiology, and biochemistry. The next phase includes pathology, microbiology, pharmacology, community medicine, and forensic medicine and toxicology. Later phases integrate medicine and allied subjects, surgery and allied subjects, paediatrics, obstetrics and gynaecology, ENT, ophthalmology, dermatology, psychiatry, orthopaedics, radiodiagnosis, anaesthesiology, and clinical postings. The curriculum also specifies two 15-day elective blocks after Phase 3 Part I examinations.
bullets: Foundation course and early clinical exposure at the start of training.
AETCOM, community engagement, and family-adoption activities run across phases.
Learning combines lectures with small-group, practical, clinical, self-directed, case-based, and community-health activities.

sections.heading: Admission process: NEET score, counselling, and allotment
paragraphs: The practical sequence is: meet the academic and age criteria; apply for and qualify in NEET (UG); obtain an All India Rank; register separately for the relevant counselling authority; submit choices; receive an allotment if eligible and ranked high enough for an available seat; then complete college-level document verification and admission formalities. The NTA conducts the examination and provides the result/rank, while counselling bodies conduct seat allotment.

For MBBS, MCC/DGHS conducts online counselling for 15% All India Quota state seats and for specified central institutions, AIIMS, JIPMER, central universities, ESIC, AFMC registration, and deemed universities. State/UT authorities conduct counselling for seats under their control, including most state quota seats. Candidates must monitor the correct portal for their target quota and college because counselling schedules, seat matrices, eligibility, domicile rules, and reporting instructions are cycle-specific.
bullets: NEET score alone does not create admission; counselling registration and choice filling are separate steps.
Use MCC for the categories it administers and the relevant state/UT counselling authority for state-controlled seats.
Check that a chosen college and its MBBS seats are listed with appropriate NMC recognition/permission status before finalising choices.

sections.heading: Costs: how to assess them safely
paragraphs: There is no single national MBBS tuition figure that applies across government, private, deemed, minority, and other institutions. Fees, deposits, hostel and mess costs, service bonds, penalties, and internship stipend arrangements are institution- and state-dependent, and may change by admission year. Compare the fee notice and prospectus for the exact seat category offered to you, and retain the official documents used for counselling and admission.

A key current safeguard is NMC’s April 7, 2026 clarification that MBBS academic fees should correspond to the 4½-year academic period, not be charged for the full five or 5½ years merely because CRMI follows. This does not eliminate other legitimate published charges, so applicants should request a written itemization and examine refund, bond, hostel, and internship policies before payment.
bullets: Treat any tuition figure as dated and college- and quota-specific.
Budget separately for application/counselling charges, tuition, hostel/mess, books/equipment, travel, examinations, and living costs.
Verify fees directly from the college/university and the applicable counselling authority—not from coaching or aggregator sites.

sections.heading: After MBBS: practice, residency, public health, and international routes
paragraphs: After completing MBBS and CRMI, graduates commonly pursue medical registration/licensure through the applicable regulatory process before independent clinical practice. NMC explains that the National Medical Register contains licensed and registered medical practitioners enrolled in State Medical Registers and entitled to practise medicine in India. Job titles such as junior resident doctor, medical officer, or general practitioner are employer- and state-specific; each recruitment notice may impose its own registration, experience, bond, reservation, and document conditions.

For Indian postgraduate clinical specialization, NEET-PG is the principal route to MD/MS and PG Diploma admissions; its eligibility conditions include a recognized MBBS qualification, appropriate registration, and completion of internship by the notified cut-off. INI-CET is the entrance route used by participating Institutes of National Importance for relevant postgraduate programmes, with each session’s AIIMS prospectus setting the detailed eligibility and deadlines. MBBS graduates may also choose non-clinical or population-health study such as an MPH; such programmes have institution-specific eligibility and selection rules rather than a single national MBBS pathway.

PLAB/UKMLA and USMLE are separate international licensing/examination pathways, not automatic consequences of an Indian MBBS. Requirements for credential verification, English-language testing, examination, clinical experience, visas, and registration should be checked directly with the UK and US regulators at the time of application.
bullets: Clinical specialization: NEET-PG, or INI-CET for participating Institutes of National Importance, followed by MD/MS/PG Diploma pathways where eligible.
Clinical employment: confirm registration/licence status and the exact employer’s recruitment rules before applying for junior-resident, medical-officer, or general-practice roles.
Public health and related fields: MPH admissions are institution-specific; for example, one current MAHE MPH programme accepts bachelor’s graduates and gives preference to health-science backgrounds.
International progression: verify requirements directly with the destination-country licensing regulator; rules and exam structures are time-sensitive.
